metadata.file_name: 75. Aydin et al. (2025). Investigating the Predictive Performance of Process Data and Result Data in Complex Problem Solving Using the Conditional Gradient Boosting Algorithm.pdf
metadata.title: Investigating the Predictive Performance of Process Data and Result Data in Complex Problem Solving Using the Conditional Gradient Boosting Algorithm

survey_design.weight_fields_interpretation: The study uses PISA 2012 Türkiye computer-based problem-solving data, restricting the analytic sample to students who answered the first item (CP025Q01) of the climate control unit and further excluding students who never clicked the required “apply” button. For the CPS score construction, the authors estimated ability using IRT models (GPCM/PCM/GRM) and explicitly included student weights in all estimations. For the machine-learning stage (conditional gradient boosting), hyperparameter tuning and model building were performed by incorporating student weights, but the manuscript does not report the exact weight variable name. No replicate-weight method (e.g., BRR/jackknife) is described.

sample_details.sample_filtering_criteria: Starting from an initial sample of 994 participants who answered the first climate control unit item (CP025Q01), the authors excluded individuals who never clicked the “apply” button (required to record meaningful exploratory actions and relationships in the process). Analyses were conducted on the remaining 915 participants. For CPS score estimation, they used students from Forms 31, 32, 35, and 36 that include the CP2 cluster containing the climate control unit.

main_findings.dataset_used: PISA 2012 Türkiye CPS (climate control unit, first item CP025Q01) — process data
main_findings.performance_metrics: Conditional gradient boosting — MAE: 50.369; MAPE: 0.103; Bias: 1.250; MSE: 3808.385; RMSE: 61.712; R: 0.742; R²: 0.551
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PISA 2012 Türkiye CPS process-data (from the first climate control item CP025Q01) to predict the CPS score, the study found that VOTAT strategy score and total time were the most influential predictors, with moderate predictive performance (R² = 0.551). The model used conditional gradient boosting with student weights and mean/median/missForest-based imputations for missing perseverance and openness variables. This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

main_findings.dataset_used: PISA 2012 Türkiye CPS (climate control unit, first item CP025Q01) — result data
main_findings.performance_metrics: Conditional gradient boosting — MAE: 45.845; MAPE: 0.095; Bias: -0.614; MSE: 3076.156; RMSE: 55.463; R: 0.774; R²: 0.599
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PISA 2012 Türkiye CPS result-data (PV1 mathematics/science/reading plus affective constructs) to predict the CPS score, the study found that mathematical literacy and reading literacy were the most influential predictors, yielding moderate-to-good predictive performance (R² = 0.599). The analysis relied on PV1 plausible values and missForest-imputed missing affective variables, with student weights incorporated during model tuning and training. This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

main_findings.dataset_used: PISA 2012 Türkiye CPS (climate control unit, first item CP025Q01) — process + result data
main_findings.performance_metrics: Conditional gradient boosting — MAE: 39.501; MAPE: 0.082; Bias: -0.758; MSE: 2278.564; RMSE: 47.734; R: 0.840; R²: 0.705
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PISA 2012 Türkiye CPS combined process+result data to predict the CPS score, the study found that mathematical literacy and VOTAT strategy score were the most influential predictors, with the best predictive performance among the three datasets (R² = 0.705). The conditional gradient boosting model incorporated student weights and used missForest-based imputations for missing perseverance/openness, but the manuscript does not report any causal identification strategy (so variable importance should not be interpreted causally). This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

data.outcome_summary: The study analyzes PISA 2012 Türkiye computer-based problem solving (climate control unit, first item CP025Q01) and predicts a model-based CPS score using conditional gradient boosting. With process data only, performance was moderate (R² = 0.551; RMSE = 61.712), with VOTAT strategy score and total time as the top predictors. With result data only (PV1 mathematics/science/reading plus perseverance and openness), performance improved to moderate-to-good (R² = 0.599; RMSE = 55.463), with mathematical literacy and reading literacy most influential. Combining process and result data yielded the best performance (R² = 0.705; RMSE = 47.734), with mathematical literacy and VOTAT strategy score most influential. Missingness in perseverance and openness was imputed using missForest after testing MCAR; student weights were incorporated during model tuning/training, and the paper does not claim causal effects from variable importance.
